Title: Overexpression of petunia chalcone isomerase in tomato results in fruit containing increased levels of flavonols

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern: In control tomato peel, endogenous chi transcript was below detection at all fruit developmental stages; in pBBC50 high-flavonol transgenics, low chi transcript detected in peel at all stages and relatively high in leaves and green/breaker/turning flesh
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Flavonol biosynthesis branch in tomato peel
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Midstream step; conversion of chalcone intermediate toward flavanone and downstream flavonols
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a likely rate-limiting step in tomato fruit peel flavonol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes isomerization of naringenin chalcone to naringenin, enabling flux into downstream flavonol formation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies immediate precursor for flavonol biosynthesis and relieves accumulation of upstream chalcone intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Fruit peel; processed tomato paste after heating/peel leaching
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant activity and potential cardiovascular health protection in human diet
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression increased peel flavonols up to 78-fold, mainly rutin/quercetin glycosides, with substantial kaempferol glycoside increase and severe depletion of naringenin chalcone
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Constitutive expression of Petunia chi in tomato strongly enhanced flavonol accumulation in fruit peel, mainly as rutin and other quercetin glycosides. 2. Hydrolyzed peel extracts showed up to 66-fold higher quercetin and 57-fold higher kaempferol than controls. 3. Naringenin chalcone was strongly depleted in high-flavonol lines, supporting increased CHI activity and flux redirection. 4. The high-flavonol phenotype cosegregated with the transgene and remained stable through at least four generations. 5. About 65% of fruit flavonols were retained after processing into paste.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression; segregation analysis across generations; HPLC metabolite profiling of hydrolyzed and nonhydrolyzed extracts; Northern blot transcript analysis; open-field evaluation; processing retention assay
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fruit developmental stage/ripening; glasshouse growth; open field trial; industrial-mimicking paste processing
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No gross vegetative phenotype difference observed; slight dullness in ripe fruit red color tone; lycopene concentration unchanged
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful metabolic engineering target to generate high-flavonol tomato fruit and processed products without major growth penalty; can be deployed in breeding/engineering for functional foods; stacking with downstream glycosylation capacity may further enhance rutin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Study experimentally manipulates heterologous Petunia chi, but metabolic phenotype is assessed in tomato. Isoquercitrin accumulation suggests rhamnosyltransferase may become limiting in high-CHI lines. Authors also discuss possible pathway-complex stabilization or feedback/feedforward effects, but these were not directly proven.
